Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

      I NTERPRETABILITY OF L ANGUAGE M ODELS                                                         FOR
002   L EARNING H IERARCHICAL S TRUCTURES
003
004
005    Anonymous authors
006    Paper under double-blind review
007
008
009                                               A BSTRACT
010
011            Transformer-based language models are effective but complex, and understanding
012            their inner workings is a significant challenge. Previous research has primarily
013            explored how these models handle simple tasks like name copying or selection,
014            and we extend this by investigating how these models grasp complex, recursive
015            language structures defined by context-free grammars (CFGs). We introduce a
016
               family of synthetic CFGs that produce hierarchical rules, capable of generating
               lengthy sentences (e.g., hundreds of tokens) that are locally ambiguous and require
017
               dynamic programming to parse. Despite this complexity, we demonstrate that
018
               generative models like GPT can accurately learn this CFG language and generate
019            sentences based on it. We explore the model’s internals, revealing that its hidden
020            states precisely capture the structure of CFGs, and its attention patterns resemble
021            the information passing in a dynamic programming algorithm.
022
023
024
      1   I NTRODUCTION
025   Transformer-based language models, like GPT (OpenAI, 2023), are powerful but mysterious; many
026   studies attempt to uncover the inner workings of transformers. Perhaps the simplest observation
027   is that attention heads can pair closing brackets with open ones, see the concurrent work and the
028   references therein (Zhang et al., 2023). Others also demonstrate that transformer can store key-value
029   knowledge pairs by storing value in the hidden embedding of keys (see Allen-Zhu & Li (2023) and
030   the references therein).
031   The seminal work from Anthropic (Elhage et al., 2021; Olsson et al., 2022) focuses on induction
032   heads, which are logic operations on the input level (such as [A][B]...[A] implies the next token
033   should be [B]). They “hypothesized” that induction heads may exist to “match and copy more ab-
034   stract and sophisticated linguistic features, rather than precise tokens”, yet they acknowledge that
035   they “don’t have a strong framework for mechanistically understanding” this.
036   The interpretability in the wild paper (Wang et al., 2022) explored many different types of attention
037   heads, including “copy head”, “name mover head”, “inhibition head”, etc. Most notably, they ex-
038   plained how GPT2 predicts the next token “Mary” given prefix “When Mary and John went to the
039   store, John gave a drink to [...]” This requires some logical reasoning by selecting (not naively copy-
040   ing) what is the right name. While this result is very inspiring, there exists very simple rule-based
041   algorithm to achieve the same.
042   In practice, transformers perform much more complex operations, yet, there is an inherent difficulty
043   in interpreting those models: To interpret how transformer performs a certain task, there must be a
044   well-defined algorithm to solve it so one can argue that the inner representations of the transformer
045   align with the algorithm. Almost all of the “impressive skills” demonstrated by state-of-the-art
046   language models are beyond solvable by any other known algorithm. Motivated by these, we ask: Is
047   there a setting for us to understand how language models perform hard tasks, involving deep logics
048   / reasoning / computation chains?
049   We propose to tackle this question in a controlled setting where the languages are generated syn-
050   thetically using context-free grammars (CFGs). CFGs, which include terminal (T) and nonterminal
051   (NT) symbols, a root symbol, and production rules, can hierarchically produce highly structured
052   expressions. A string is part of CFG language if a rule sequence can transform the root symbol into
053   this string, and the language model is asked to complete the given partial strings from the CFG.
      We pick CFG because, there exists textbook-level, yet quite difficult dynamic programming (DP)

059   Figure 1: An example CFG used in our experiments. It generates long (e.g., length 354 in this example) and
060             ambiguous strings. Determining if a string x belongs to the CFG typically requires dynamic pro-
                gramming, even when the CFG rules are known.
061
062
063   algorithm to solve CFG instances.1 Generally,
064    • We wish to capture long-range dependencies via CFG. The simplest example is bracket match-
065      ing, in ...Y(...)[[...]{...}]{...}X, the next symbol X could depend on Y that was
066      hundreds of tokens before. Another example is coding, where goto jumpback can only be
067      used if jumpback is a valid line number that could be hundreds of lines ago.
068    • We wish to capture local ambiguity. A coding grammar (like python) can be parsed using greedy
069      without ambiguity, so does bracket matching — once locally seen ...()... we know the two
070      parentheses must be paired together. We focus on hard CFGs that require global planning via
071      dynamic programming to parse.
072
      Most popular choices of CFGs do not satisfy the two above properties. Notably, the English CFG
073   (e.g., derived from Penn TreeBank) has an average length of 28 tokens (too short), and is not
074   very locally ambiguous (e.g., RB JJ or JJ PP imply their parent must be ADJP). As we show
075   in Appendix I, such CFGs can even be learned using tiny GPT2 models with ∼ 100k parameters.
076   Thus, it is too easy for our interpretability purpose.
077
      For such reason, we design our own synthetic CFG languages. We give one example in Figure 1
078
      and discuss a family of 7 such CFGs with varying difficulties in Section 2 (we have 15 more in the
079   appendix).2 We pre-train GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), denoted by GPT for short, on a language
080   modeling task using a large corpus of strings sampled from our constructed CFGs. We test the
081   model’s accuracy and diversity by feeding it prefixes from the CFG (or no prefix, just the starting
082   token) and observing if it can generate accurate completions.
083
      It is perhaps evident from Figure 1 that even if the CFG tree is given, deciding if the string belongs
084
      to this language for a real person may require a scratch paper and perhaps half an hour, not to say to
085   learn such CFG from scratch. However, we demonstrate that GPT can learn such CFGs, and using
086   rotary or relative attentions is crucial, especially for complex CFGs (Results 1-3). Additionally, we
087   examine attention patterns and hidden states to understand how GPT achieves this. Specifically, we:
088
089    • Results 4-5. Develop a multi-head linear probing method to verify that the model’s hidden states
090
         linearly encode NT information almost perfectly, a significant finding as pre-training does not
         expose the CFG structure. (In contrast, encoder models like BERT do not.)
091
       • Results 6-9. Introduce methods to visualize and quantify attention patterns, demonstrating that
092
         GPT learns position-based and boundary-based attentions, contributing to understanding how it
093
         learns CFG’s regularity, periodicity, and hierarchical structure.
094
       • Corollary. Suggest that GPT models learn CFGs by implementing a dynamic programming-like
095      algorithm. The boundary-based attention allows a token to attend to its closest NT symbols in
096      the CFG tree, even when separated by hundreds of tokens. This resembles DP, in which the CFG
097      parsing on a sequence 1...i needs to be “concatenated” with another sequence i + 1...j in order
098      to form a solution to a larger problem on 1...j. See Figure 2+8 for illustrations.
099
      In Appendix B, we also explore implicit CFGs (Post & Bergsma, 2013), where each T symbol is a
100
      bag of words, and show that GPT simply learns to encode the word information on its embedding
101
      layer. We also investigate model robustness using CFGs, showcasing under what conditions the
102   model can auto-correct errors and generate valid CFGs from a corrupted prefix (e.g., randomly
103   flipping 15% of the symbols in the prefix). These results are numbered 10 through 13.
104
105      1
           Not to say in the theory community, CFGs are also used to model some rich, recursive structure in lan-
106   guages, including some logics, grammars, formats, expressions, patterns, etc.
         2
107        A benefit of using synthetic data is to control the difficulty of the data, so that we can observe how trans-
      formers learn to solve tasks at different difficulty levels, and observe their difference.

119
120   2                O UR S YNTHETIC C ONTEXT-F REE G RAMMARS
121
122   A probabilistic context-free grammar (CFG) is a formal system defining a string distribution using
123   production rules. It comprises four components: terminal symbols (T), nonterminal symbols (NT),
124
      a root symbol (root ∈ NT), and production rules (R). We represent a CFG as G = (T, NT, R),
      with L(G) denoting the string distribution generated by G.
125
126   We mostly focus on L-level CFGs where each level ℓ ∈ [L] corresponds to a set of symbols NTℓ
127   with NTℓ ⊆ NT for ℓ < L, NTL = T, and NT1 = {root}. Symbols at different levels
128   are disjoint: NTi ∩ NTj = ∅ for i ̸= j. We consider rules of length 2 or 3, denoted as R =
129   (R1 , . . . , RL−1 ), where each Rℓ consists of rules in the form:
130                           r = (a 7→ b, c, d) or                          r = (a 7→ b, c) for                             a ∈ NTℓ                     and b, c, d ∈ NTℓ+1
131
      Given a non-terminal symbol a ∈ NT      and any rule r = (a 7→ ⋆), we say a ∈ r. For each a ∈ NT,
132
      its associated set of rules is R(a) := r | r ∈ Rℓ ∧ a ∈ r , its degree is |R(a)|, and the CFG’s size
133
      is (|NT1 |, |NT2 |, . . . , |NTL |).
134
135   Generating from CFG. To generate samples x from L(G), follow these steps:
136
      1. Start with the root symbol NT1 .
137
      2. For each layer ℓ < L, keep a sequence of symbols sℓ = sℓ,1 , · · · , sℓ,mℓ .
138
139
      3. For the next layer, randomly sample a rule r ∈ R(sℓ,i ) for each sℓ,i with uniform probability.3
         Replace sℓ,i with b, c, d if r = (sℓ,i 7→ b, c,   d), or with b, c if r = (sℓ,i 7→ b, c). Let the resulting
140
         sequence be sℓ = sℓ+1,1 , · · · , sℓ+1,mℓ+1 .
141
142
      4. During generation, when a rule sℓ,i 7→ sℓ+1,j , sℓ+1,j+1 is applied, define the parent parℓ+1 (j) =
         parℓ+1 (j + 1) := i (and similarly if the rule of sℓ,i is of length 3).
143
      5. Define NT ancestor indices p = (p1 (i), . . . , pL (i))i∈[mL ] and NT ancestor symbols s =
144
         (s1 (i), . . . , sL (i))i∈[mL ] as shown in Figure 2:
145
146                                             pL (j) := j ,                pℓ (j) := parℓ+1 (pℓ+1 (j)) and                                             sℓ (j) := sℓ,pℓ (j)
147
148
      The final string is x = sL = (sL,1 , · · · , sL,mL ) with xi = sL,i and length len(x) = mL . We
      use (x, p, s) ∼ L(G) to represent x with its associated NT ancestor indices and symbols, sampled
149
      according to the generation process. We write x ∼ L(G) when p and s are evident from the context.
150
151   Definition 2.1. A symbol xi in a sample (x, p, s) ∼ L(G) is the NT boundary / NT end at level
152
      ℓ ∈ [L − 1] if pℓ (i) ̸= pℓ (i + 1) or i = len(x). We denote bℓ (i) := 1xi is the NT boundary at level ℓ as the
      NT-end boundary indicator function. The deepest NT-end of i is
153
154                                             b♯ (i) = minℓ∈{2,3,...,L−1} {bℓ (i) = 1}                                              or ⊥ if set is empty .
155
156   The cfg3 synthetic CFG family. We focus on seven synthetic CFGs of depth L = 7 detailed
157   in Section C.1. The hard datasets cfg3b, cfg3i, cfg3h, cfg3g, cfg3f have sizes (1, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3) and
158   increasing difficulties cfg3b < cfg3i < cfg3h < cfg3g < cfg3f. The easy datasets cfg3e1 and
159      3
           For simplicity, we consider the uniform case, eliminating rules with extremely low probability. Such rules
160   complicate the learning of the CFG and the investigation of a transformer’s inner workings (e.g., require larger
161   networks and longer training time). Our results do extend to non-uniform cases when the distributions are not
      heavily unbalanced.

7        6        10        4        6        6


167    (b) a family of max-depth 11 CFGs where rules have length 1 or 2 that GPT can learn, see cfg0 in Appendix I
168
      Figure 3: CFG visual comparisons: left is a medium-length sample, and right is a 80%-percentile-length sample
169
170
171
      cfg3e2 have sizes (1, 3, 9, 27, 81, 27, 9) and (1, 3, 9, 27, 27, 9, 4) respectively. The sequences gen-
172
      erated by these CFGs are up to 36 = 729 in length. Typically, the learning difficulty of CFGs
173   inversely scales with the number of NT/T symbols, assuming other factors remain constant, be-
174   cause having more NT/T symbols makes the language less ambiguous and more easily parsed
175   using greedy (see Figure 4 and we discuss more in Appendix I). We thus primarily focus on
176   cfg3b, cfg3i, cfg3h, cfg3g, cfg3f.
177
      Why Such CFGs. We use CFG as a proxy to study some rich, recursive structure in languages,
178
      which can cover some logics, grammars, formats, expressions, patterns, etc. Those structures are
179   diverse yet strict (for example, Chapter 3.1 should be only followed by Chapter 3.1.1, Chapter 4 or
180   Chapter 3.2, not others). The CFGs we consider are non-trivial, with likely over 2270 > 1080 strings
181   in cfg3f among a total of over 3300 > 10140 possible strings of length 300 or more (see the entropy
182   estimation in Figure 4). In particular, Figure 30 in the appendix shows that cfg3f cannot be learned
183   by transformers (much) smaller than GPT2-small. In contrast, the English CFG (e.g., derived from
184   Penn TreeBank) can be learned to good accuracy using tiny GPT2 models with ∼ 100k parameters
185   — so it is too easy for our interpretability purpose.
186   To obtain the cleanest interpretability result, we have selected a CFG family with a “canonical rep-
187   resentation” (e.g., a layered CFG). This controlled design choice allows us to demonstrate a strong
188   correlation between the CFG representation and the hidden states in the learned transformer. We also
189   create additional CFG families to examine “not-so-canonical” CFG trees, with results deferred to
190   Appendix I (see an example in Figure 3). We do not claim our results encompass all CFGs; our cho-
191   sen CFGs are already quite challenging for a transformer to learn and can lead to clean hierarchical
192   interpretability results.
193
194   3    R ESULTS 1-3: T RANSFORMER C AN L EARN S UCH CFG S
195
196   In this section, we generate a large corpus {x(i) }i∈[N ] from a synthetic CFG language L(G) in
197
      Section 2, and pretrain a (generative, decoder-only) transformer model F on this corpus, treating
      each terminal symbol as a separate token, using an auto-regressive task (see Appendix C.3 for de-
198
      tails). We then evaluate how well the model learns such L(G).
199
200   Models. We denote the GPT2 small architecture (12-layer, 12-head, 768-dimensions) as GPT (Rad-
201   ford et al., 2019) and implemented its two modern variants. We denote GPT with relative positional
202   attention (He et al., 2020) as GPTrel , and GPT with rotary positional embedding (Su et al., 2021;
203
      Black et al., 2022) as GPTrot . For purposes in later sections, we introduce two weaker variants.
      GPTpos replaces the attention matrix with a matrix based solely on tokens’ relative positions, while
204
      GPTuni uses a constant, uniform average of past tokens from various window lengths as the attention
205
      matrix. Detailed explanations of these variants are in Section C.2.
206
207   We quickly summarize our findings and then elaborate them in details.
208   Result 1-3 (Figure 4). The GPT models can effectively learn our synthetic CFGs. Given any prefix,
209   they can generate completion strings
210
       • that can perfectly adhere to the CFG rules most of the time,                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         (accuracy)
211
212    • that are sufficiently diverse in the CFG language, and                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          (diversity)
213    • that closely follow the probabilistic distribution of the CFG language.                                                                                                       (probability)
214
215   Moreover, one had better use rotary or relative attentions; the original GPT (with absolute positional
      embedding) performs even worse than GPTuni (with uniform attention).

                                                                                                                             e2    256    252    255     251      253    252                           e2   0.00031   0.00025    0.00025    0.00011   0.00011
220
      Figure 4: Generation accuracy (left), entropy (middle), KL-divergence (right) across multiple CFG datasets.
221
                Observations: Less ambiguous CFGs (cfg3e1, cfg3e2, as they have fewer NT/T symbols) are easier
222             to learn. Transformers using relative positional embedding (GPTrel or GPTpos ) are better for learning
223             harder CFGs. The vanilla GPT is worse than even GPTuni , which is GPT with fixed, uniform attentions.
224
225
226   Result 1: Completion accuracy. We evaluate F by letting it generate completions for prefixes
227   x:c = (x1 , x2 , · · · , xc ) from strings x freshly sampled from L(G). The generation accuracy is
228   measured as Prx∼L(G) + randomness of F [(x:c , F (x:c )) ∈ L(G)]. We use multinomial sampling without
229   beam search for generation.4 Figure 4 (left) shows the generation accuracies for cuts c = 0 and
230   c = 50. The c = 0 result tests the transformer’s ability to generate a sentence in the CFG, while
231
      c = 50 tests its ability to complete a sentence.5 The results show that the pretrained GPT models
      can often generate strings that perfectly adhere to the CFG rules for the cfg3 data family.
232
233   Result 2: Generation diversity. Could it be possible that the pretrained GPT models only mem-
234   orized a small subset of strings from the CFG? We evaluate this by measuring the diversity of its
235   generated strings. High diversity suggests a better understanding of the CFG rules.
236   We consider two methods to estimate diversity. One is to estimate the distribution’s entropy, which
237   provides a rough estimate of (the log2 of) the support size, see the middle of Figure 4. The other is
238   to use birthday paradox to theoretically lower bound the support size (Arora & Zhang, 2017). This
239   allows us to make precise claims, such as in the cfg3f dataset, there are at least 4 × 108 distinct
240   sentential forms derivable from a symbol at levels 1 to 5 or levels 2 to 6; not to say from the root to
241   level 7. Details are in Appendix D. Our general conclusion is that the pre-trained model does not
242   rely on simply memorizing a small set of patterns to achieve high completion accuracy.
243   Result 3: Distribution comparison. To fully learn a CFG, it is crucial to learn the distribution
244   of generating probabilities. One naive approach is to compare the marginal distributions p(a, i), for
245   the probability of symbol a ∈ NTℓ appearing at position i. We observe a strong alignment between
246   the generation probabilities and the ground-truth, included in Appendix D.2. Another approach
247   is to compute the KL-divergence between the per-symbol conditional distributions. Let p∗ be the
248   distribution
                   over strings in the true CFG and p be that from the generative transformer model. Let
249   S = x(i) i∈[M ] be samples from the true CFG distribution. Then, the KL-divergence can be
250   estimated as follows:6
251         1
              P          1
                             P                                                                                                P                                               Prp∗ [t|x1 ,...,xi−1 ]
                                                                                                                                   t∈T∪{eos} Prp [t | x1 , . . . , xi−1 ] log Prp [t|x1 ,...,xi−1 ]
                                                                                                                                                ∗
                     |S|                        x∈S len(x)+1                           i∈[len(x)+1]
252
253   In Figure 4 (right) we compare the KL-divergence between the true CFG distribution and the GPT
254   models’ output distributions using M = 20000 samples.
255   Connection to DP. Result 1-3 (e.g., learning the CFG’s marginal distribution) is merely an small
256   step towards showing that the model employs a DP-like approach. Dynamic programming (e.g., the
257   inside-outside algorithm Baker (1979)) can compute marginal distributions of CFGs, and such al-
258   gorithms can be implemented using nonlinear neural networks like transformers, achieving a global
259   minimum in the auto-regressive training objective.7 However, the mere existence of a dynamic-
260   programming transformer to obtain the training objective’s global minimum is not entirely satisfac-
261   tory. Does employing an AdamW stochastic optimizer for 100k iterations on the training objective
262   yield such an algorithm? The remainder of this paper will delve deeper to address this question.
263
264      4
            The last softmax layer converts the model outputs into a probability distribution over (next) symbols.
265
      We follow this distribution to generate the next symbol, reflecting the unaltered distribution learned by the
266   transformer. This is the source of the “randomness of F ” and is often referred to as using “temperature τ = 1.”
267       5
            Our cfg3 family is large enough to ensure a negligible chance of a freshly sampled prefix of length 50
268   being seen during pretraining.
          6
269         A nearly identical formula was also used in DuSell & Chiang (2022).
          7
            This has been carefully explored for masked language modeling case in Zhao et al. (2023).

                column represents the accuracy of predicting sℓ , the NT ancestors at level ℓ, via linear probing (4.2).
276
277              It also encodes NT boundaries (Appendix E.1); and such information is discovered gradually and
278              hierarchically across layers and training epochs (Appendix E.2 and E.3). We compare against a
279              baseline which is the encoding from a randomly-intialized GPT, GPTrand (serving as a neural-tangent
280              kernel baseline). We also compare against DeBERTa, illustrating that BERT-like models are less
                 effective in learning NT information at levels close to the CFG root.
281
282
283
284
      4       R ESULTS 4-5: H OW D O T RANSFORMERS L EARN CFG S ?
285   In this section, we delve into the learned representation of the transformer to understand how it
286   encodes CFGs. We employ various measurements to probe the representation and gain insights.
287
288
      Recall classical way to solve CFGs. Given CFG G, the classical way to verify if a sequence
      x satisfies L(G) is to use dynamic programming (DP) (Sakai, 1961; Sipser, 2012). One possible
289
      implementation of DP involves using the function DP(i, j, a), which determines whether or not
290
      xi+1 , xi+1 . . . , xj can be generated from symbol a following the CFG rules. From this DP repre-
291
      sentation, a DP recurrent formula can be easily derived.8
292
293   In the context of this paper, any sequence x ∼ L(G) that satisfies the CFG must satisfy the following
294   conditions:
295            bℓ (i) = 1, bℓ (j) = 1, ∀k ∈ (i, j), bℓ (k) = 0 and sℓ (j) = a =⇒ DP(i, j, a) = 1                                                                                                                                        (4.1)
296
      (recall the NT-boundary bℓ and the NT-ancestor sℓ notions from Section 2). Note that (4.1) is not
297
      an “if and only if” condition because there may be a subproblem DP(i, j, a) = 1 that does not lie
298
      on the final CFG parsing tree but is still locally parsable by some valid CFG subtree. However,
299   (4.1) provides a “backbone” of subproblems, where verifying their DP(i, j, a) = 1 values certifies
300   that the sentence x is a valid string from L(G). It is worth mentioning that there are exponentially
301   many implementations of the same DP algorithm9 and not all (i, j, a) tuples need to be computed
302   in DP(i, j, a). Only those in the “backbone” are necessary.
303
      Connecting to transformer. In this section, we investigate whether pre-trained transformer F
304
      also implicitly encodes the NT ancestor and boundary information. If it does, this suggests that
305   the transformer contains sufficient information to support all the DP(i, j, a) values in the backbone.
306   This is a significant finding, considering that transformer F is trained solely on the auto-regressive
307   task without any exposure to NT information. If it does encode the NT information after pretraining,
308   it means that the model can both generate and certify sentences in the CFG language.
309
310   4.1     R ESULT 4: T RANSFORMER ’ S L AST L AYER E NCODES NT A NCESTORS /B OUNDARIES
311
      Let l be the last layer of the transformer (other layers are studied in Appendix E.2). Given
312
      an input string x, we denote the hidden state of the transformer at layer l and position                  i as
313
      Ei (x) ∈ Rd . We first investigate whether a linear function can predict b1 (i), . . . , bL (i) i∈[len(x)]
314
315
      and s1 (i), . . . , sL (i) i∈[len(x)] using the full Ei (x) i∈[len(x)] . If possible, it implies that the last-
316   layer hidden states encode the CFG’s structural information up to a linear transformation.
317       8
            For example, one can compute DP(i, j, a) = 1 if and only if there exists i = i1 < i2 < · · · < ik = j
318
      such that DP(ir , ir+1 , br ) = 1 for all r ∈ [k − 1] and a → b1 , b2 , . . . , bk is a rule of the CFG. Implementing
319   this naively would result in a O(len4 ) algorithm for CFGs with a maximum rule length of 3. However, it can
320   be implemented more efficiently with O(len3 ) time by introducing auxiliary nodes (e.g., via binarization).
          9
321         Each inner loop of the dynamic programming can proceed in any arbitrary order, not limited to k = i..j or
                                                                                                                 2
322   k = j..i, and the algorithm can prune and break early. This gives a safe estimate of at least (n!)Ω(n ) possible
323   implementations. Furthermore, there are at least 2Ω(n) ways to perform binarization, meaning to break length-3
      rules to length-2 ones. This is just to detect if a given string of length n belongs to the CFG.

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

                the NT ancestor and boundary information in the red boxes very well. (They may not encode NT

                ancestors for smaller levels because that may not be information-theoretically possible.)

335   Multi-head linear probing (full). Due to the high dimensionality of this linear function (e.g.,
336
      len(x) = 300 and d = 768 yield 300 × 768 dimensions) and variable string lengths, we propose
      a multi-head linear function for efficient learning. We consider a set of linear functions fr : Rd →
337
      R|NT| , where r ∈ [H] and H is the number of “heads”. To predict any sℓ (i), we apply:
338
                            Gi (x) = r∈[H],k∈[len(x)] wr,i→k · fr (Ek (x)) ∈ R|NT|
                                     P
339                                                                                                    (4.2)
340                                                                                                                                                      ′
                                             exp(⟨Pi,r ,Pk,r ⟩)
341   where wr,i→k := P ′                                                            for trainable parameters Pi,r ∈ Rd . Gi can be seen as
                                       k ∈[len(x)] exp(⟨Pi,r ,Pk′ ,r ⟩)
342   a “multi-head attention”   over linear functions. We train Gi (x) ∈ R|NT| using the cross-entropy loss

      to predict sℓ (i) ℓ∈[L] . Despite having multiple heads,

347     L
                                                                     not on x. Similarly, we train Gi (x) ∈

      R using the logistic loss to predict the binary values bℓ (i) ℓ∈[L] . Details are in Section C.4.
348
349   Using such multi-head linear probing, we discover that:
350
      Result 4 (Figure 5). Pre-training allows GPT models to almost perfectly encode the NT an-
351
      cestor sℓ (i) and NT boundary bℓ (i) information in the last transformer layer’s hidden states
352   (Ek (x))k∈[len(x)] , up to a linear transformation. In contrast, encoder models (like deBERTa) may
353
      not learn deep NT information very well.10
354
355   But, do we need this full layer for linear probing? We explore next.
356
357   4.2   R ESULT 5: NT A NCESTORS ARE E NCODED AT NT B OUNDARIES
358
359   Above, we used the full hidden layer, Ei (x) i∈[len(x)] , to predict sℓ (i) ℓ∈[L] for each position i.
360   This is essential since it’s information-theoretically impossible to extract all of i’s NT ancestors
361   by only reading Ei (x) or even all hidden states to its left, especially if xi is the start of a string or
362   a subtree in the CFG. But, how about those ones information-theoretically possible? In particular,
363
      how about predicting sℓ (i) at locations i with bℓ (i) = 1 — i.e., at the end of the CFG subtrees.
364   Multi-head linear probing (diagonal). We consider a neighborhood of position i in the hidden
365   states, say Ei±1 (x), and use that for linear probing. In symbols, we replace wr,i→k in (4.2) with
366   zeros for |i − k| > 1 (tridiagonal masking), or with zeros for i ̸= k (diagonal masking).
367
           Gi (x) = r∈[H],k∈[len(x)],|i−k|≤δ wr,i→k · fr (Ek (x)) ∈ R|NT|
                     P
                                                                                 where δ = 0 or 1   (4.3)
368
369
      Result 5 (Figure 6). For GPT models, the information of position i’s NT ancestor/boundary is
370   locally encoded around position i ± 1 when i is on the NT boundary. This is because:
371
372    • At NT boundaries (i.e., bℓ (x) = 1), diagonal or tridiagonal multi-head linear probing (4.3) is
373
         adequate for accurately predicting the NT ancestors sℓ (x) (see Figure 9 on Page 13).
374      10
            Among encoder-based models, deBERTa (He et al., 2020) is a modern variant of BERT, which is equipped
375   with relative attentions. It is expected that encoder-based models do not learn very deep NT information,
376   because in a masked-language modeling (MLM) task, the model only needs to figure out the missing token
377   from its surrounding, say, 20 tokens. This can be done by pattern matching, as opposed to a global planning
      process like dynamic programming.

                      Figure 7: After pretrained on our CFG data, GPT model’s attention layers have a strong bias towards “ NT-end
389
                                at level ℓ′ to the most adjacent NT-end at ℓ ”, for even different ℓ, ℓ′ . For definitions see Section 5.2,

395
396
                               • Such masking is also sufficient for accurately predicting NT boundaries bℓ (i) (deferred to
397
                                 Figure 18 in Appendix E.1).
398
399                   In contrast, encoder models like deBERTa do not store deep NT information at the NT boundaries.
400
                      Related work. Our probing approach is akin to the seminal work by Hewitt & Manning (2019),
401
                      which uses linear probing to examine the correlation between BERT’s hidden states and the parse
402
                      tree distance metric (similar to NT-distance in our language). Subsequent studies (Shi et al., 2022;
403                   Zhao et al., 2023; Maudslay & Cotterell, 2021; Manning et al., 2020; Vilares et al., 2020; Wu et al.,
404                   2020; Arps et al., 2022) have explored various probing techniques to suggest that BERT-like trans-
405                   formers can approximate CFGs from natural languages.
406
                      Our approach differs in that we use synthetic data to demonstrate that linear probing can almost per-
407
                      fectly recover NT ancestors and boundaries, even for complex CFGs that generate strings exceeding
408
                      hundreds of tokens. We focus on pre-training generative (decoder-only) language models. For a
409                   non-generative, encoder-based model like BERT (Kenton & Toutanova, 2019) or its modern variant
410                   deBERTa (He et al., 2020), they do not learn deep (i.e., close to the CFG root) NT information very
411                   well, as shown in Result 4-5.
412
                      Our results, along with Section 5, provide evidence that generative language models like GPT-2 em-
413
                      ploy a dynamic-programming-like approach to generate CFGs, while encoder-based models, typi-
414
                      cally trained via MLM, struggle to learn more complex/deeper CFGs.
415
416
417
                      5                           R ESULTS 6-9: H OW D O T RANSFORMERS L EARN NT S ?
418                   We now delve into the attention patterns. We demonstrate that these patterns mirror the CFG’s
419                   syntactic structure and rules, with the transformer employing different attention heads to learn NTs
420                   at different CFG levels.
421
422                   5.1                               R ESULT 6: P OSITION -BASED ATTENTION
423
                      We first note that the transformer’s attention weights are primarily influenced by the tokens’ relative
424
                      distance. This holds true even when trained on the CFG data with absolute positional embedding.
425
                      This implies that the transformer learns the CFG’s regularity and periodicity through positional in-
426                   formation, which it then uses for generation. (We defer the figures to Appendix F.1 as this finding
427                   may not surprise some readers.) Motivated by this, we explore whether using position-based atten-
428                   tion is sufficient to learn CFGs. In Figure 4, we find that GPTpos (or even GPTuni ) performs well, sur-
429                   passing the vanilla GPT, but not reaching the full potential of GPTrel . This supports the superior prac-
430                   tical performance of relative-position based transformer variants (such as GPTrel , GPTrot , deBERTa)
431                   over their base models (GPT or BERT). On this other hand, this also indicates that position-based
                      attention alone is not enough for transformers to learn CFGs.


                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        8
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


432
      5.2    R ESULT 7-9: B OUNDARY-BASED ATTENTION
433
434   Next, we remove the position-bias from the attention matrix to examine the remaining part. We find
435   that the transformer also learns a strong boundary-based attention pattern, where tokens on the NT-
436   end boundaries typically attend to the “most adjacent” NT-end boundaries, see Figure 2. This
437   attention pattern enables the transformer to effectively learn the hierarchical and recursive structure
438
      of the CFG, and generate output tokens based on the NT symbols and rules.
439   Formally, let Al,h,j→i (x) for j ≥ i denote the attention weight for positions j → i at layer l and
440   head h of the transformer, on input sequence x. Given a sample pool {x(n) }n∈[N ] ∈ L(G), we
441   compute for each layer l, head h,11
442
443           Al,h,p = AverageJAl,h,j→i (x(n) ) | n ∈ N, 1 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ len(x(n) ) s.t. j − i = pK ,
444   which represents the average attention between any token pairs of distance p over the sample pool.
445   To remove position-bias, we focus on Bl,h,j→i (x) := Al,h,j→i (x) − Al,h,j−i in this subsection. Our
446   observation can be broken down into three steps.
447   Result 7 (Figure 7(a)). Bl,h,j→i (x) exhibits a strong bias towards tokens i at NT ends.
448
449   This can be seen in Figure 7(a), where we present the average value of Bl,h,j→i (x) over data x and
450   pairs i, j where i + δ is the deepest NT-end at level ℓ (symbolically, b♯ (i + δ) = ℓ). The attention
451   weights are highest when δ = 0 and decrease rapidly for surrounding tokens.
452   Result 8 (Figure 7(b)). Bl,h,j→i (x) favors pairs i, j both at NT ends at some level ℓ.
453
454   This can be seen in Figure 7(b), where we show the average Bl,h,j→i (x) over data x and pairs i, j
455   where bℓ (i + δ1 ) = bℓ (j + δ2 ) = 1 for δ1 , δ2 ∈ {−1, 0, 1}. It is maximized when δ1 = δ2 = 0.
456   Result 9 (Figure 7(c)). Bl,h,j→i (x) favors “adjacent” NT-end token pairs i, j .
457
                                                                   end→end
458   Above, we define “adjacency” as follows. We introduce Bl,h,ℓ     ′ →ℓ,r to represent the average value

459   of Bl,h,j→i (x) over samples x and token pairs i, j that are at the deepest NT-ends on levels ℓ, ℓ′
460   respectively (symbolically, b♯ (i) = ℓ ∧ b♯ (j) = ℓ′ ), and are at a distance r based on the ancestor
                                                                                    end→end
461   indices at level ℓ (symbolically, pℓ (j) − pℓ (i) = r). We observe that Bl,h,ℓ    ′ →ℓ,r decreases as r
                                                                 ′
462   increases, and is highest when r = 0 (or r = 1 for pairs ℓ → ℓ without an r = 0 entry).12
463
464
      In conclusion, tokens corresponding to NT-ends at level ℓ′ statistically have higher attention weights
      to their most adjacent NT-ends at every level ℓ, even after removing position-bias.13
465
466
      5.3    C ONNECTION TO DP
467
468   Dynamic programming (DP) comprises two components: storage and recurrent formula. Identify-
469   ing a specific DP implementation that a transformer follows is challenging due to the “exponentially
470   many” ways to implement such DPs (see Footnote 9). However, we highlight common elements in
471   all DP implementations and their correlation with the transformer. In Section 4, we demonstrated
472   that transformers can encode the DP’s storage “backbone”, encompassing all necessary DP(i, j, a)
473
      on the correct CFG parsing tree, regardless of the DP implementation.
474   For the recurrent formula, consider DP(k, j, a) in the backbone, derived from DP(k, i, b) ∧
475   DP(i, j, c) using CFG rule a 7→ b, c. Given that DP(k, i, b) is stored near position i while DP(k, j, a)
476   and DP(i, j, c) are stored near position j (Result 5), the model needs to perform a memory read of
477   position i from position j, or j → i. Note that positions i and j are adjacent NT-ends of the same
478   level, and we have verified that GPT models favor attending j → i when i and j are adjacent NT-
479
      ends, serving as evidence that (decoder-only) transformers use a DP-like approach. See Figure 8
      (top) for an illustration.
480
481     11
            Throughout this paper, we use J·K to denote multi-sets that allow multiplicity, such as J1, 2, 2, 3K. This
482   allows us to conveniently talk about its set average.
483      12
            For any token pair j → i with ℓ = b♯ (i) ≥ b♯ (j) = ℓ′ — meaning i is at an NT-end closer to the root than
484   j — it satisfies pℓ (j) − pℓ (i) ≥ 1 so their distance r is strictly positive.
         13
485         Without removing position-bias, such a statement might be meaningless as the position-bias may favor
      “adjacent” anything, including NT-end pairs.

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

491                  8 8 8 8 8 9 9 9 9 9 7 7 7 9 9 8 8 9 9 7 7 7 9 9 8 8 8 9 9 9 9 7 7 7 ...


492               𝑥= 1   2   3   3   1   3   3    1    2   1      2     2   1   1    1    1   2    1   1   3   1   2      1   1    3    3     1   1    1     1   1    2   2   1 ...

                                                                                                                                                                                                           learns to
493                                                                                               positions 𝑖                                      position 𝑗
                                                                                                                                                                                                           generate
494                                                                                                                                                                                                        from CFG

495                                      𝐷𝑃2 𝑡, 15 = 𝐷𝑃 0, 𝑖1 , 𝟏𝟑                                     𝐷𝑃(𝑖1 , 𝑖2 , 𝟏𝟎)
                                                                                                                                                                                  𝐷𝑃2 (𝑗, 𝑎) = whether symbol 𝑎
496                                                                   𝐷𝑃2 (𝑡, 𝟏𝟎)                                                 𝐷𝑃(𝑖2 , 𝑗, 𝟖)
                                                                                                                                                                                   can follow sequence 𝑥1 … 𝑥𝑗
                                                                             𝐷𝑃2 (𝑡, 𝟗)
497
            Figure 8: Illustration of how GPTs mimic dynamic programming. See discussions in Section 5.3.
498
499
500
501
502   Further reading for experts. Transformers are not only parsing algorithms but also generative
503   ones. Experts in CFGs (or experienced participants in coding competitions) may immediately un-
504
      derstand that the generative process requires implementing a second DP:
505       let DP2 (j, a) denote if prefix x1 , . . . , xj can be followed with a given symbol a ∈ NT ∪ T.
506
      Suppose there is a rule b 7→ c, a, and DP(i, j, c) ∧ DP2 (i, b) both hold; this implies DP2 (j, a) also
507
      holds. This is analogous to the inside-outside algorithm (Baker, 1979), and is a special case of
508   problem 6 in the IOI 2006 competition. In this case, the model also needs to perform a memory read
509   of position i from position j. Here, position i is the most adjacent NT-end to position j at a different
510   level; we have also verified that GPT models favor attending such j → i. See Figure 8 (bottom).
511
      Finally, the above demonstration shows how to correctly parse and generate, but to generate follow-
512
      ing the same distribution of CFGs, the model needs to learn DP′2 (j, a), the probability that symbol
513
      a can follow prefix x1 , . . . , xj . The recurrent formula is similar in terms of memory read patterns
514   (thus the attention patterns). We ignore this subtlety for conciseness.
515
516   In sum, while identifying a specific DP implementation that a transformer learns is nearly impossi-
517
      ble, we have shown that the backbone of the DP — including the necessary DP storage states and
      recurrent formula — are observable in the pretrained models’ hidden states and attention patterns.
518
      This serves as strong evidence that pretrained (decoder-only) transformers largely mimic dynamic
519
      programming, regardless of the specific DP implementation they choose.
520
521
522
      6    R ELATED W ORK AND C ONCLUSION
523   We defer implicit CFGs and robust CFGs to Appendix B.
524
525
      Transformers can encode some CFGs, especially those that correspond to natural languages (He-
      witt & Manning, 2019; Shi et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023; Maudslay & Cotterell, 2021; Manning
526
      et al., 2020; Vilares et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020; Arps et al., 2022). Deletang et al. (2023) stud-
527
      ied transformer’s learnability on a few languages in the Chomsky hierarchy (which includes CFGs)
528   However, the inner mechanisms regarding how transformer can or cannot solve those tasks are un-
529   clear. There are works “better” than us by precisely interpreting each neuron’s function, but they
530   study simpler tasks using simpler architectures. For instance, Nanda et al. (2023) examined 1 or
531   2-layer transformers with context length 3 for the arithmetic addition. In addition to linear probing,
532   Murty et al. (2023) explored alternative methods to deduce the tree structures learned by a trans-
533   former. They developed a score to quantify the “tree-like” nature of a transformer, demonstrating
534   that it becomes increasingly tree-like during training. Our Figure 20 in Appendix E.3 also confirmed
535   on such findings. (This paper appears in May 2023, so we focus on related works before that.)
536   Conclusion. We studied how transformers learn synthetically generated, yet challenging CFGs,
537   and show the inner workings correlate with the internal states of the dynamic programming algo-
538   rithms needed to parse such CFGs. We hope this will point towards more opportunities towards
539   understanding larger models on complex tasks. (Indeed, we are writing a series of papers using the
      findings and probing techniques developed from this paper; we cannot cite them due to anonymity.)

709   Figure 10: Language models learn implicit CFGs by using word embeddings to encode the (hidden) terminal
710              symbol.
711
                We present word embedding correlations for GPT pre-trained on an implicit CFG with |T| = 3 and
712             vocabulary size |OT| = 300. There are 300 rows/columns each representing an observable token
713             a ∈ OT. Label ijk ∈ {0, 1}3 in the figure indicates whether a is in OTt for the three choices
714             t ∈ T. Details are in Section B.1.
715
716
717
718   B     R ESULTS 10-13: E XTENSIONS OF CFG S
719
      B.1   R ESULT 10: I MPLICIT CFG S
720
721   In an implicit CFG, terminal symbols represent bags of tokens with shared properties. For exam-
722   ple, a terminal symbol like noun corresponds to a distribution over a bag of nouns, while verb
723   corresponds to a distribution over a bag of verbs. These distributions can be non-uniform and over-
724   lapping, allowing tokens to be shared between different terminal symbols. During pre-training, the
725   model learns to associate tokens with their respective syntactic or semantic categories, without prior
726
      knowledge of their specific roles in the CFG.
727   Formally, we consider a set of observable tokens OT, and each terminal symbol t ∈ T in
728   G is associated with a subset OTt ⊆ OT and a probability distribution Dt over OTt . The
729   sets (OTt )t can be overlapping. To generate a string from this implicit CFG, after generating
730   x = (x1 , x2 , . . . , xm ) ∼ L(G), for each terminal symbol xi , we independently sample one element
731   yi ∼ Dxi . After that, we observe the new string y = (y1 , y2 , · · · , ym ), and let this new distribution
732
      be called y ∼ LO (G)
733   We pre-train language models using samples from the distribution y ∼ LO (G). During testing, we
734   evaluate the success probability of the model generating a string that belongs to LO (G), given an
735   input prefix y:c . Or, in symbols,
736

                                 Pry∼LO (G)+randomness of F (y:c , F (y:c )) ∈ LO (G) ,
737
738   where F (y:c ) represents the model’s generated completion given prefix y:c . (We again use dynamic
739   programming to determine whether the output string is in LO (G).)
740   We summarize our finding below and deferring details to Appendix G.
741    Result 10 (Figure 10). Generative language models can learn implicit CFGs very well. In par-
742    ticular, after pretraining, the token embeddings from the same subset OTt are grouped together,
743    indicating they use token embedding layer to encode the hidden terminal symbol information.
744
745
      B.2   R ESULTS 11-13: ROBUSTNESS ON C ORRUPTED CFG
746
747   One may also wish to pre-train a transformer to be robust against errors and inconsistencies in
748   the input. For example, if the input data is a prefix with some tokens being corrupted or missing,
749   then one may hope the transformer to correct the errors and still complete the sentence following
750   the correct CFG rules. Robustness is an important property, as it reflects the generalization and
751   adaptation ability of the transformer to deal with real-world training data, which may not always
752
      follow the CFG perfectly (such as having grammar errors).
753   To test robustness, for each input prefix x:c of length c that belongs to the CFG, we randomly select a
754   set of positions i ∈ [c] in this prefix — each with probability ρ — and flip them i.i.d. with a random
755   symbol in T. Call the resulting prefix x e:c . Next, we feed the corrupted prefix x
                                                                                        e:c to the transformer F
      and compute its generation accuracy in the uncorrupted CFG: Prx∼L(G), F [(x:c , F (e       x:c )) ∈ L(G)].


                                                                                                           14
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


756                                                                                                            --------------------pre-training method--------------------
                                                                       NT-level 0.1 random perturbation                  T-level 0.15 random perturbation               NT-level 0.05 deterministic permutation

763   Figure 11: Generation accuracies for models pre-trained cleanly VS pre-trained over perturbed data, on clean
764              or corrupted prefixes with cuts c = 0 or c = 50, using generation temperatures τ = 0.1, 0.2, 1.0.
765
766                                              Observation. In Rows 4/5, by comparing against the last column, we see it is beneficial to include
                                                 low-quality data (e.g. grammar mistakes) during pre-training. The amount of low-quality data
767
                                                 could be little (γ = 0.1 fraction) or large (every training sentence may have grammar mistake). The
768                                              transformer also learns a “mode switch” between the “correct mode” or not; details in Section B.2.
769
770
      We not only consider clean pre-training, but also some versions of robust pre-training. That is, we
771   randomly select γ ∈ [0, 1] fraction of the training data and perturb them before feeding into the
772   pre-training process. We compare three types of data perturbations.14
773
774
       • (T-level random perturbation). Each xi w.p. 0.15 we replace it with a random symbol in  T.
775    • (NT-level random perturbation). Let ℓ = L − 1 and recall sℓ = sℓ,1 , sℓ,2 , . . . , sℓ,mL−1 is the
776
         sequence of symbols at NT-level ℓ. For each sℓ,i , w.p. 0.10 we perturb it to a random symbol in
         NTℓ ; and then generate x = sL according to this perturbed sequence.
777
778
       • (NT-level deterministic perturbation). Let ℓ = L − 1 and fix a permutation π over symbols in
         NTℓ . For each sℓ,i , w.p. 0.05 we perturb it to its next symbol in NTL−1 according to π; and
779
         then generate x = sL according to this perturbed sequence.
780
781   We focus on ρ = 0.15 with a wide range of perturbation rate τ = 0.0, 0.1, . . . , 0.9, 1.0. We present
782   our findings in Figure 11. The main message is:
783    Result 11 (Figure 11, rows 4/5). When pretrained over clean data, GPT models are not so robust
784    to “grammar mistakes.” It is beneficial to include corrupted or low-quality pretrain data.
785
786   Specifically, GPT models achieve only ∼ 30% accuracy when pretrained over clean data x ∼ L(G).
787
      If we pretrain from perturbed data — both when γ = 1.0 so all data are perturbed, and when
      γ = 0.1 so we have a small fraction of perturbed data — GPT can achieve ∼ 79%, 82% and 60%
788
      robust accuracies respectively using the three types of data perturbations (rows 4/5 of Figure 11).
789
790   Next, we take a closer look. If we use temperature τ = 1 for generation:
791    Result 12 (Figure 11, rows 3/6/9). Pre-training on corrupted data teaches model a mode switch.
792
         • Given a correct prefix, it mostly completes with a correct string in the CFG (Row 9);
793
794      • Given a corrupted prefix, it always completes sentences with grammar mistakes (Row 6);
795      • When given no prefix, it generates corrupted strings with probability close to γ (Row 3).
796
797   By comparing the generation accuracies across different τ and γ, we observe:
798    Result 13 (Figure 11, rows 4/5/6). High robust accuracy is achieved when generating using low
799    temperatures τ ,15 and is not sensitive to γ – the fraction of pretrain data that is perturbed.
800
801   This should not be surprising given that the language model learned a “mode switch.” Using low
802   temperature encourages the model to, for each next token, pick a more probable solution. This
803   allows it to achieve good robust accuracy even when the model is trained totally on corrupted data
804   (γ = 1.0). Note this is consistent with practice: when feeding a pre-trained completion model (such
805     14
            One can easily extend our experiments by considering other types of data corruption (for evaluation), and
806
      other types of data perturbations (for training). We refrain from doing so because it is beyond the scope of this
807   paper.
808      15
            Recall, when temperature τ = 0 the generation is greedy and deterministic; when τ = 1 it reflects the
809   unaltered distribution learned by the transformer; when τ > 0 s small it encourages the transformer to output
      “more probable” tokens.


                                                                                                                               15
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


810
      as Llama or GPT-3-davinci003) with prompts of grammar mistakes, it tends to produce texts also
811   with (even new!) grammar mistakes when using a large temperature.
812
813   Our experiments suggest that, additional instruct fine-tuning may be necessary, if one wants the
814
      model to always stay in the “correct mode” even for high temperatures. This is beyond the scope of
      this paper.
815
816
817   C     E XPERIMENT S ETUPS
818
      C.1   DATASET D ETAILS
819
820   We construct seven synthetic CFGs of depth L = 7 with varying levels of learning difficulty. It can
821   be inferred that the greater the number of T/NT symbols, the more challenging it is to learn the CFG.
822   For this reason, to push the capabilities of language models to their limits, we primarily focus on
823   cfg3b, cfg3i, cfg3h, cfg3g, cfg3f, which are of sizes (1, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3) and present increasing levels
824   of difficulty. Detailed information about these CFGs is provided in Figure 12:
825     • In cfg3b, we construct the CFG such that the degree |R(a)| = 2 for every NT a. We also ensure
826       that in any generation rule, consecutive pairs of T/NT symbols are distinct.
827       The 25%, 50%, 75%, and 95% percentile string lengths are 251, 278, 308, 342 respectively.
828     • In cfg3i, we set |R(a)| = 2 for every NT a. We remove the requirement for distinctness to make
829       the data more challenging than cfg3b.
830
          The 25%, 50%, 75%, and 95% percentile string lengths are 276, 307, 340, 386 respectively.
831
        • In cfg3h, we set |R(a)| ∈ {2, 3} for every NT a to make the data more challenging than cfg3i.
832
833
          The 25%, 50%, 75%, and 95% percentile string lengths are 202, 238, 270, 300 respectively.
834     • In cfg3g, we set |R(a)| = 3 for every NT a to make the data more challenging than cfg3h.
835       The 25%, 50%, 75%, and 95% percentile string lengths are 212, 258, 294, 341 respectively.
836     • In cfg3f, we set |R(a)| ∈ {3, 4} for every NT a to make the data more challenging than cfg3g.
837       The 25%, 50%, 75%, and 95% percentile string lengths are 191, 247, 302, 364 respectively.
838   Remark C.1. From the examples in Figure 12, it becomes evident that for grammars G of depth 7,
839   proving that a string x belongs to L(G) is highly non-trivial, even for a human being, and even when
840   the CFG rules are known. The standard method of demonstrating x ∈ L(G) is through dynamic
841   programming. We further discuss what we mean by a CFG’s “difficulty” in Appendix I, and provide
842   additional experiments beyond the cfg3 data family.
843   Remark C.2. cfg3f is a dataset that sits right on the boundary of difficulty at which GPT2-small is
844   capable of learning, see Figure 30 later which shows that smaller GPT2 cannot learn such cfg3f (and
845   refer to subsequent subsections for training parameters). While it is certainly possible to consider
846   deeper and more complex CFGs, this would necessitate training a larger network for a longer period.
847
      We choose not to do this as our findings are sufficiently convincing at the level of cfg3f.
848   Simultaneously, to illustrate that transformers can learn CFGs with larger |NT| or |T|, we construct
849   datasets cfg3e1 and cfg3e2 respectively of sizes (1, 3, 9, 27, 81, 27, 9) and (1, 3, 9, 27, 27, 9, 4). They
850   are too lengthy to describe so only included in the supplementary materials.
851
852   C.2   M ODEL A RCHITECTURE D ETAILS
853
854
      We define GPT as the standard GPT2-small architecture (Radford et al., 2019), which consists of 12
      layers, 12 attention heads per layer, and 768 (=12 × 64) hidden dimensions. We pre-train GPT on
855
      the aforementioned datasets, starting from random initialization. For a baseline comparison, we also
856
      implement DeBERTa (He et al., 2020), resizing it to match the dimensions of GPT2 — thus also
857   comprising 12 layers, 12 attention heads, and 768 dimensions.
858
859   Architecture size. We have experimented with models of varying sizes and observed that their
860
      learning capabilities scale with the complexity of the CFGs. To ensure a fair comparison and en-
      hance reproducibility, we primarily focus on models with 12 layers, 12 attention heads, and 768
861
      dimensions. The transformers constructed in this manner consist of 86M parameters.
862
863   Modern GPTs with relative attention. Recent research (He et al., 2020; Su et al., 2021; Black
      et al., 2022) has demonstrated that transformers can significantly improve performance by using

887   Figure 12: The context-free grammars cfg3b, cfg3i, cfg3h, cfg3g, cfg3f that we primarily use in this paper,
888              together with a sample string from each of them.
889
                          Observation. Although those CFGs are only of depth 7, they are capable of generating sufficiently
890
                          long and hard instances; after all, even when the CFG rules are given, the typical way to decide if a
891                       string x belongs to the CFG language x ∈ L(G) may require dynamic programming.
892
893
894   attention mechanisms based on the relative position differences of tokens, as opposed to the absolute
895   positions used in the original GPT2 (Radford et al., 2019) or BERT (Kenton & Toutanova, 2019).
896   There are two main approaches to achieve this. The first is to use a “relative positional embedding
897   layer” on |j − i| when calculating the attention from j to i (or a bucket embedding to save space).
898
      This approach is the most effective but tends to train slower. The second approach is to apply a
      rotary positional embedding (RoPE) transformation (Su et al., 2021) on the hidden states; this is
899
      known to be slightly less effective than the relative approach, but it can be trained much faster.
900
901   We have implemented both approaches. We adopted the RoPE implementation from the GPT-NeoX-
902   20B project (along with the default parameters), but downsized it to fit the GPT2 small model. We
903   refer to this architecture as GPTrot . Since we could not find a standard implementation of GPT using
904
      relative attention, we re-implemented GPT2 using the relative attention framework from DeBERTa
      (He et al., 2020). (Recall, DeBERTa is a variant of BERT that effectively utilizes relative positional
905
      embeddings.) We refer to this architecture as GPTrel .
906
907   Weaker GPTs utilizing only position-based attention. For the purpose of analysis, we also
908   consider two significantly weaker variants of GPT, where the attention matrix exclusively depends
909   on the token positions, and not on the input sequences or hidden embeddings. In other words, the
910
      attention pattern remains constant for all input sequences.
911   We implement GPTpos , a variant of GPTrel that restricts the attention matrix to be computed solely
912   using the (trainable) relative positional embedding. This can be perceived as a GPT variant that
913   maximizes the use of position-based attention. We also implement GPTuni , a 12-layer, 8-head, 1024-
914   dimension transformer, where the attention matrix is fixed; for each h ∈ [8], the h-th head consis-
915   tently uses a fixed, uniform attention over the previous 2h − 1 tokens. This can be perceived as a
916
      GPT variant that employs the simplest form of position-based attention.
917   Remark C.3. It should not be surprising that GPTpos or GPTuni perform much worse than other GPT
      models on real-life wikibook pre-training. However, once again, we use them only for analysis


                                                                                                                17
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


918
      purpose in this paper, as we wish to demonstrate what is the maximum power of GPT when only
919   using position-based attention to learn CFGs, and what is the marginal effect when one goes beyond
920   position-based attention.
921
922   Features from random transformer. Finally we also consider a randomly-initialized GPTrel , and
923   use those random features for the purpose of predicting NT ancestors and NT ends. This serves as a
924
      baseline, and can be viewed as the power of the so-called (finite-width) neural tangent kernel (Jacot
      et al., 2018; Allen-Zhu et al., 2019). We call this GPTrand .
925
926
      C.3    P RE -T RAINING D ETAILS
927
928   For each sample x ∼ L(G) we append it to the left with a BOS token and to the right with an
929   EOS token. Then, following the tradition of language modeling (LM) pre-training, we concatenate
930   consecutive samples and randomly cut the data to form sequences of a fixed window length 512.
931   As a baseline comparison, we also applied DeBERTa on a masked language modeling (MLM) task
932   for our datasets. We use standard MLM parameters: 15% masked probability, in which 80% chance
933   of using a masked token, 10% chance using the original token, and 10% chance using a random
934   token.
935
      We use standard initializations from the huggingface library. For GPT pre-training, we use AdamW
936   with β = (0.9, 0.98), weight decay 0.1, learning rate 0.0003, and batch size 96. We pre-train the
937   model for 100k iterations, with a linear learning rate decay.16 For DeBERTa, we use learning rate
938   0.0001 which is better and 2000 steps of learning rate linear warmup.
939
      Throughout the experiments, for both pre-training and testing, we only use fresh samples from the
940
      CFG datasets (thus using 4.9 billion tokens = 96 × 512 × 100k). We have also tested pre-training
941
      with a finite training set of 100m tokens; and the conclusions of this paper stay similar. To make
942   this paper clean, we choose to stick to the infinite-data regime in this version of the paper, because
943   it enables us to make negative statements (for instance about the vanilla GPT or DeBERTa, or about
944   the learnability of NT ancestors / NT boundaries) without worrying about the sample size. Please
945   note, given that our CFG language is very large (e.g., length 300 tree of length-2/3 rules and degree
946   4 would have at least 4300/3 possibility), there is almost no chance that training/testing hit the same
947   sentence.
948
      As for the reproducibility of our result, we did not run each pre-train experiment more than once (or
949
      plot any confidence interval). This is because, rather than repeating our experiments identically, we
950   find it more interesting to use the resources to run it against different datasets and against different
951   parameters. We pick the best model using the perplexity score from each pre-training task. When
952   evaluating the generation accuracy in Figure 4, we have generated more than 20000 samples for
953   each case, and present the diversity pattern accordingly in Figure 13.
954
955   C.4    P REDICT NT ANCESTOR AND NT BOUNDARY
956
      Recall from Section 4.1 that we have proposed to use a multi-head linear function to probe whether
957
      or not the hidden states of a transformer, implicitly encodes the NT ancestor and NT boundary
958   information for each token position. Since this linear function can be of dimension 512 × 768 —
959   when having a context length 512 and hidden dimension 768 — recall in (4.2), we have proposed
960   to use a multi-head attention to construct such linear function for efficient learning purpose. This
961   significantly reduces sample complexity and makes it much easier to find the linear function.
962
      In our implementation, we choose H = 16 heads and hidden dimension d′ = 1024 when construct-
963
      ing this position-based attention in (4.2). We have also tried other parameters but the NT ances-
964   tor/boundary prediction accuracies are not very sensitive to such architecture change. We again use
965   AdamW with β = (0.9, 0.98) but this time with learning rate 0.003, weight decay 0.001, batch size
966   60 and train for 30k iterations.
967
      Once again we use fresh new samples when training such linear functions. When evaluating the
968
      accuracies on predicting the NT ancester / boundary information, we also use fresh new samples.
969
      Recall our CFG language is sufficiently large so there is negligible chance that the model has seen
970
        16
971        We have slightly tuned the parameters to make pre-training go best. We noticed for training GPTs over our
      CFG data, a warmup learning rate schedule is not needed.

       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

988
                                   Ω(M 2 ) to level ℓ2 = 6 (not to say to the T-level ℓ2 = 7); this is already more than the number
989                                of parameters in the model. Therefore, we conclude that the pre-trained model does not rely on
990                                simply memorizing a small set of patterns to learn the CFGs.
